note:
跳到主要内容我在做科技记者的时候就一直在看彩色电子阅读器的原型。回到Verge的早期，我们经常会介绍像高通的Mirasol或各种E Ink原型这样的显示技术，然后在离开时会想，虽然它还没有完全准备好，但这只是个时间问题。这就是技术的工作方式，对吗？我们在一次贸易展上看到了它未完成的形式，几年后它出现在主流产品中。
不用说，事实并非如此。亚马逊和Kobo仍然没有销售彩色电子阅读器，而且如果有什么不同的话，那就是前景似乎比十年前更遥远了，因为现在可以以远低于100美元的价格出售还可以的LCD平板电脑。它们没有E Ink的电池续航时间或户外可读性，但很明显，这是一个利基问题。
不过，E Ink仍在研究这个想法，今年该公司推出了最适合电子阅读器的彩色屏幕技术：Kaleido。我一直在测试首批使用这种显示屏的电子阅读器之一，新款售价199欧元(234美元)的Pocket Book Color，虽然它不是一款完美的设备，但它足够好，让我对彩色E Ink的未来抱有希望。
Pockbook是一家总部设在瑞士的电子阅读器制造商，主要销往各个欧洲市场。该公司成立于2007年，自称是全球第三大高端电子墨水电子阅读器制造商，大概仅次于亚马逊(Amazon)和乐天(Rakuten)的Kobo。
撇开屏幕不谈，Pocket Book Color看起来与其他任何6英寸的电子阅读器没有太大不同。它都是塑料的，但设计相当流畅，背面有哑光银色，前面有柔软的触感。您将在底部边缘找到一个电源按钮、一个微型USB端口(BOO)和一个microSD插槽(Yay)。有一个捆绑的微型USB到耳机插孔适配器，这样你就可以听有声读物了，而且该设备还支持无线耳机的蓝牙。
屏幕下方有主页、菜单和翻页按钮，所有这些按钮都很容易操作；160克的Pocket Book Color足够轻，拇指放在底部。实际上，与我以前拥有的Kindle的侧装翻页按钮相比，我更喜欢这个按钮，这些按钮从来没有感觉到它们的高度是正确的-至少对我的拇指来说不是这样。
Pocketbook Color运行基于Linux的定制操作系统，其中包含各种应用程序。有一个网络浏览器，一个笔记应用程序，甚至还有一些游戏，比如国际象棋和数独。翻页和菜单性能是合理的，尽管没有我的Kindle OASIS那么快。电池续航时间非常长--我已经几个星期没有给Pocket Color充电了。
所有这些都足够好了，但如果不是在屏幕上，Pockbook Color将是一款完全不起眼的设备。那么让我们来谈谈屏幕吧。
Kaleido屏幕的关键要素是，E Ink开发了比其旧的Triton技术使用的更薄、质量更高的滤色器阵列，后者需要在单色面板的顶部使用基于玻璃的滤色器层。E Ink表示，Kaleido显示器可以显示高达4096种颜色和16级灰度，这与其声称的Triton相同，但整体色彩再现能力有了很大改善。
问题是滤色器阵列严重降低了屏幕的整体分辨率，因为它位于单色E Ink微囊的前面。Pocket Book Color的6英寸屏幕在显示黑白内容时的分辨率为1072×1448，这使得像素密度高达300ppi，但显示彩色的屏幕区域的像素密度降至100。如果你仔细观察，在阅读单色内容时也可以看到滤镜，使屏幕看起来比现代传统电子阅读器略有颗粒感。
在亮度、对比度或活力方面，Kaleido屏幕显然无法与平板电脑竞争。但我喜欢在同样的情况下使用Pocket Book Color，我更喜欢使用Kindle而不是iPad。在阳光下，屏幕在户外看起来是最好的-我会把彩色复制品比作几天后褪色的报纸。不完全是令人惊叹的，但肯定是可读性和无眩光的。在实际操作中，分辨率也不是什么大问题，因为在正常的观看距离下，文本仍然清晰，图像看起来也很好。
在室内，屏幕就不那么令人印象深刻了，因为你真的需要使用前灯才能看到很多东西。把它调得太高，你会觉得自己不再像在看电子书阅读器了，但在亮度太低的情况下，颜色很难辨认出来。我发现将亮度设置在30%左右通常能提供最佳的色彩和舒适性平衡。但是，由于家里的眩光和耗电不那么令人担忧，我更有可能首先去买一台普通的平板电脑。
总体而言，我认为Kaleido并不完美，但至少作为一种电子阅读器屏幕技术是可行的，而Pocket Book Color是我见过的最好的彩色电子阅读器。但是它最大的问题是我不知道怎么处理它。
Pockbook商店的英语内容选择极其有限。我不会为此太严厉地批评这款产品，因为它甚至没有在英语国家的市场上销售，但如果你选择进口，你应该预料到必须用你自己的不含DRM的内容来加载它。Pocketbook Color支持几种图书格式，包括EPUB、CBR、CBZ、MOBI和PDF，您可以通过Dropbox或PocketBook自己的云服务将文件同步到设备，这是简单而快速的。
但是，即使在一个口袋商店里堆满了畅销书的世界里，或者在你有合法的、无DRM的拷贝你想读的一切的世界里，口袋颜色也不是最好的充分利用Kaleido屏幕的方式。基本上，它太小了。杂志和漫画书看起来不错，但在6英寸的显示屏上看起来太难了。漫画是一个很好的选择，但往往是压倒性的黑白。偶尔带有彩色插图或插图的普通书籍相当不错，但这并不是一个非常常见或令人兴奋的使用案例，而且单色电子阅读器的整体屏幕质量更好。
有缩放、缩放和查看单个面板的模式，但我觉得它们不是很方便或有效。虽然Pocket Book Color的性能总体上还不错，但它并不能真正胜任处理大型PDF的任务。我真的会对这款设备的8或9英寸屏幕感兴趣，因为我认为它更适合在Kaleido显示屏上工作最好的内容类型。
有一件事在钱包颜色上确实看起来很棒，那就是书籍封面，你知道他们是怎么评价这些书籍的。不过，如果说有什么不同的话，那就是这可能是推动该技术采用的原因。看一看你的图书馆和书店的彩色效果绝对不错--甚至连E Ink本身都把Kaleido卖给你，称它能够“更充分地实现电子书购物体验”。如果有什么能让亚马逊加入的话，你会认为那就是它。
颜色E墨水还没有完全到位。尽管Pocket Book Color是迄今为止同类设备中最好的，但考虑到它的价格几乎是Kindle PaperWhite的两倍，要比传统的电子阅读器更好地推荐它是很难的，除非你知道你想在上面读什么。你是否已经有了一台6英寸的电子阅读器，并且经常用它来阅读你希望能用彩色阅读的东西？这可能值得一查。你在等一个完美的漫画阅读设备吗？不是这个。
我不认为Kaleido目前的形式是将彩色E墨水作为主流的技术，但它正在实现这一目标。尽管彩色再现永远无法与LCD平板电脑竞争，但具有同等(或理想情况下更高)清晰度的更大屏幕将非常适合在户外阅读漫画和杂志等东西，而且电池续航优势依然显著。
Kaleido彩色E油墨之所以好，原因与单色E油墨相同。尽管如此，它仍然有很多权衡之处，而口袋颜色并不是展示其真正潜力的设备。但请密切关注这项技术--很长一段时间以来，人们第一次感觉彩色电子阅读器可能很快就会出现。
note_en:
Skip to main content                                                                                                                I’ve been looking at color e-reader prototypes as long as I’ve been a tech journalist. Back in the early days of  The Verge, we’d often cover display technology like Qualcomm’s  Mirasol or various  E Ink prototypes and come away thinking that while it wasn’t quite ready, it was just a matter of time. That’s how technology is meant to work, right? We see it in unfinished form at a trade show and it turns up in mainstream products a few years later.
 Needless to say, that has not turned out to be the case. Amazon and Kobo still don’t sell color e-readers, and if anything the prospect seems even further off than it did a decade ago, now that it’s possible to sell passable LCD tablets for well under $100. They don’t have the battery life or the outdoor readability of E Ink, but it’s clear that that’s a niche concern.
 E Ink is still working on the idea, though, and this year the company shipped its best shot yet at color screen tech that’s appropriate for e-readers: Kaleido. I’ve been testing one of the first e-readers to use this type of display, the new €199 ($234) PocketBook Color, and while it’s not a perfect device, it’s good enough to give me hope in the future of color E Ink.
  PocketBook is an e-reader manufacturer headquartered in Switzerland and mostly selling to various European markets. The company has been around since 2007 and says it’s the world’s third largest maker of premium E Ink-based e-readers, presumably putting it behind Amazon and Rakuten’s Kobo.
 Screen aside, the PocketBook Color doesn’t look much different to any other 6-inch e-reader. It’s all plastic, but the design is fairly sleek with a matte silver finish on the back and a soft-touch texture on the front. You’ll find a power button, a Micro USB port (boo), and a microSD slot (yay) on the bottom edge. There’s a bundled Micro USB-to-headphone jack adapter so you can listen to audiobooks, and the device also has Bluetooth support for wireless headphones.
 There are home, menu, and page-turn buttons located below the screen, and they’re all easily accessible; at 160g, the PocketBook Color is light enough to hold with your thumb resting at the bottom. I actually prefer this to the side-mounted page-turn buttons on previous Kindles I’ve owned, which never quite felt like they were at the right height — at least not for my thumbs.
 The PocketBook Color runs a custom Linux-based OS that includes various apps. There’s a web browser, a notes app, and even some games like chess and sudoku. Page-turn and menu performance is reasonable, if not quite as fast as my Kindle Oasis. Battery life has been very good — I haven’t charged the PocketBook Color in weeks.
 All of this is nice enough, but the PocketBook Color would be an entirely unremarkable device if not for the screen. So let’s talk about the screen.
   The key element of Kaleido screens is that E Ink has developed a thinner, higher quality color filter array than it used in its older Triton technology, which required a glass-based color filter layer on top of the monochrome panel. E Ink says Kaleido displays can show up to 4,096 colors and 16 levels of grayscale, which is the same as it claimed for Triton, but the overall color reproduction is much improved.
 The catch is that the color filter array severely reduces the screen’s overall resolution, since it sits in front of the monochrome E Ink microcapsules. The PocketBook Color’s 6-inch screen has a resolution of 1072 × 1448 when displaying black and white content, which makes for a high pixel density of 300 ppi, but areas of the screen that show color see pixel density drop to 100. The filter is also visible when reading monochrome content if you look closely, giving the screen a slightly grainier look than modern conventional e-readers.
  Kaleido screens obviously aren’t going to compete with tablets in brightness, contrast, or vibrancy. But I like using the PocketBook Color in the same situations where I’d prefer to use a Kindle over an iPad. The screen looks its best outdoors in the sun — I’d compare the color reproduction to a newspaper that’s faded over a few days. Not exactly stunning, then, but certainly readable and glare-free. The resolution isn’t a big problem in practice, either, because the text remains sharp and images look fine at normal viewing distances.
 The screen is less impressive indoors, because you really need to use the front light to see much of anything. Crank it up too high and you don’t really feel like you’re looking at an e-reader any more, but the colors are hard to make out at too low a brightness. I found setting it to around 30-percent brightness usually offered the best balance of color and comfort. But with glare and power consumption less of a concern at home, I’d be more likely to reach for a regular tablet in the first place.
 Overall, I think Kaleido is imperfect but at least viable as an e-reader screen technology, and the PocketBook Color is by far the best color e-reader I’ve ever seen. But its biggest problem is that I just didn’t know what to do with it.
   PocketBook’s store has an extremely limited selection of English-language content. I’m not going to criticize the product too hard for that, since it isn’t even sold in Anglophone markets, but you should expect to have to load it up with your own DRM-free content if you choose to import. The PocketBook Color supports several book formats including EPUB, CBR, CBZ, MOBI, and PDF, and you can sync files to the device either over Dropbox or through PocketBook’s own cloud service, which is simple and fast.
  But even in a world where the PocketBook store was stacked with bestsellers, or where you had legal, DRM-free copies of everything you’d ever want to read, the PocketBook Color wouldn’t be the best way to make the most of a Kaleido screen. Basically, it’s too small. Magazines and comic books look good, but they’re just too hard to read on a 6-inch display. Manga is a good fit, but tends to be overwhelmingly in black and white. Regular books with the occasional color illustration or graphic work pretty well, but that’s not a very common or exciting use case, and monochrome e-readers have better overall screen quality.
 There are modes for zooming, scaling, and viewing individual panels, but I didn’t find them to be very convenient or effective. While the PocketBook Color’s performance is generally fine, it isn’t really up to the task of manipulating a large PDF. I would be really interested in a version of this device with an 8- or 9-inch screen, because I think it’d be a much better fit for the type of content that works best on the Kaleido display.
 The one thing that does reliably look great on the PocketBook Color is book covers, and you know what they say about judging books on those. If anything, though, that could be the thing that drives adoption of the technology. It’s definitely nice to look at your library and the book store in color — even E Ink itself  sells Kaleido as enabling “a more fully realized ebook shopping experience.” If anything’s going to get Amazon on board, you’d think it would be that.
   Color E Ink isn’t quite there yet. Even though the PocketBook Color is the best device of its kind to date, it’s hard to recommend over traditional e-readers unless you know what you want to read on it, considering it’s almost twice the price of a Kindle Paperwhite. Do you have a 6-inch e-reader already and often use it for things that you wish you could read in color? This might be worth checking out. Are you waiting for a perfect comic-reading device? This isn’t it.
 I don’t think Kaleido in its current form is the technology that’s going to take color E Ink mainstream, but it’s getting there. Even though the color reproduction is never going to compete with LCD tablets, a bigger screen with equal (or ideally higher) sharpness would be great for reading things like comics and magazines outdoors, and the battery life advantage remains significant.
 Kaleido color E Ink is good for the same reasons as monochrome E Ink. It still comes with a lot of trade-offs, though, and the PocketBook Color isn’t the device that will show off its true potential. But keep an eye on this technology — for the first time in a long while, it actually feels like color e-readers could be viable soon.
title: 袖珍彩色评论：彩色E墨水迄今最好的屏幕，电池续航时间长，但对于彩色内容来说太小了，与单色搭配看起来不太好
title_en: PocketBook Color review: best screen yet for color E Ink and has good battery life, but is too small for color content and doesn't look good with monochrome

note:
数据保护和数据管理技术开发商Veeam Software周一宣布收购Kasten，Kasten是一家为Kubernetes(本地工作负载)提供备份和灾难恢复技术的开发商。
Veeam首席技术官兼产品战略高级副总裁丹尼·艾伦(Danny Allan)表示，此举正值IT行业看到保护基于集装箱的工作负载的需求激增之际。Veeam最近将其全球总部迁至俄亥俄州哥伦布市。
艾伦援引研究公司企业战略集团(Enterprise Strategy Group)的统计数据称，到2023年，预计约50%的工作量将转移到集装箱上。
“集装箱会留在这里，”艾伦告诉CRN。“我们看到容器的采用速度越来越快，就像我们在采用VMware时看到的那样。VMware本身正在将Kubernetes构建到其坦桑尼亚技术中。这将进一步加速采用。“。
Allan说，虽然大多数开发保护基于容器的数据的技术的公司都是从存储的角度来处理这个问题，但Kasten是不同的。
“卡斯滕从应用的角度看待容器，”他说。这与Veeam如何将自己打造成一家价值10亿美元的公司是一致的。当Veeam出来的时候，每个人都在用特工。但是，当数据保护转移到虚拟化环境时，将重点放在应用程序上就变得很重要。Veeam使用以应用程序为中心的方式迁移数据，无需代理。“。
他说：“该软件是作为Kubernetes软件部署的，因此这是一种非常本地化的体验。”我们将保持这种本地DevOps体验，以便在云和内部部署环境中提供可见性。“。
艾伦说，Veeam从今年年初开始与Kasten建立合作伙伴关系，两人利用上周的VMworld会议展示了他们的技术集成。他说，这两家公司都归Insight Partners所有，这有助于促进收购。
“这证明了我们在这个领域的投资，”他说。他说：“我们已经是虚拟化和云环境中的数据保护领先者。”“我们正在转向‘第2幕’，它将重点放在云和容器上。”
艾伦说，对Veeam来说，收购Kasten而不是继续合作是很重要的。
他表示：“集装箱市场的发展速度决定了我们采取行动确保在这个市场上的领先地位。”卡斯滕在以应用为中心的容器领域处于领先地位。我们将保持这种领导地位。“。
通过收购Kasten，Veeam成为最新一家在保护Kubernetes基于容器的工作负载方面进行重大投资的主要存储供应商。
Pure Storage上个月表示，将以3.7亿美元全现金交易收购Portworx。Pure Storage将Portworx描述为领先的Kubernetes数据服务平台的开发商，用于在生产中的容器中运行任务关键型工作负载。
数据保护和数据管理软件开发商CommVault在7月份推出了其HyperScale X存储设备，该设备使用广泛使用的容器存储接口(CSI)来保护基于Kubernetes的有状态容器。
NetApp今年早些时候放弃了自己的基于容器的存储技术开发，转而支持Project Astra，该公司表示，该项目的目标是将持久性存储与Kubernetes Containers统一起来。
集装箱仓储也成为投资标的。今年2月，总部位于加利福尼亚州圣何塞的Kubernetes环境容器附加存储开发商MayaData获得了2,600万美元的投资，其中包括佛罗里达州劳德代尔堡(Fort Lauderdale)的软件定义存储技术开发商DataCore。
note_en:
Data protection and data management technology developer Veeam Software Monday unveiled the acquisition of Kasten, a developer of backup and disaster recovery technology for Kubernetes-native workloads.
 The move comes as the IT industry is seeing an explosion in the need to protect container-based workloads, said Danny Allan, chief technology officer and senior vice president of product strategy at Veeam, which recently moved its worldwide headquarters to Columbus, Ohio.
  Allan, citing statistics from research firm Enterprise Strategy Group, said about 50 percent of workloads are expected to be moved to containers by 2023.
 “Containers are here to stay,” Allan told CRN. “We’re seeing an increasingly fast adoption of containers similar to what we saw with the adoption of VMware. And  VMware itself is building Kubernetes into its Tanzu technology. This will further acceleration adoption.”
 While most companies developing technology for protecting container-based data approach the problem from a storage point of view, Kasten is different, Allan said.
 “Kasten looks at containers from the application point of view,” he said. “This aligns with how Veeam has built itself into a billion-dollar company. When Veeam came out, everybody was using agents. But when data protection moved to virtualized environments, it became important to focus on the applications. Veeam used an application-centric way to migrate data without the need for agents.”
  “The software is deployed as Kubernetes software, so it is a very native experience,” he said. “We will maintain that native DevOps experience to give visibility in cloud and on-premises environments.”
 Veeam has had a partnership with Kasten starting early this year, and the two used last week’s  VMworld conference to demonstrate their technical integration, Allan said. Both companies are owned by Insight Partners, which helped facilitate the acquisition, he said.
  “This is a testament to our investment in this space,” he said. “We are already the data protection leader in virtualized and cloud environments,” he said. “We are moving to ‘Act 2,’ which will be a focus on cloud and containers.”
 It was important for Veeam to acquire Kasten instead of continuing its partnership, Allan said.
 “The speed at which the container market is evolving dictated we move to ensure leadership in this market,” he said. “And Kasten has leadership in application-focused containers. We will maintain that leadership.”
 With its acquisition of Kasten, Veeam becomes the latest major storage vendor to make a significant investment in protecting Kubernetes container-based workloads.
 Pure Storage last month said it is acquiring Portworx in a $370 million all-cash deal. Pure Storage described Portworx as the developer of a leading Kubernetes data services platform for running mission-critical workloads in containers in production.
 Data protection and data management software developer  Commvault in July unveiled its HyperScale X storage appliances that use the widely used container storage interface, or CSI, that protects Kubernetes-based stateful containers.
 NetApp earlier this year abandoned its own container-based storage technology development in favor of  Project Astra, which it said was targeted at uniting persistent storage with Kubernetes containers.
 Container storage has also become an investment target. MayaData, a San Jose, Calif.-based developer of container-attached storage for Kubernetes environments, in February received a  $26 million investment that included among the investors DataCore, a Fort Lauderdale, Fla.-based developer of software-defined storage technology.
title: 云数据管理提供商Veeam Software以1.5亿美元收购Kasten，Kasten为Kubernetes原生工作负载开发备份和灾难恢复技术
title_en: Cloud data management provider Veeam Software acquires Kasten, which develops backup and disaster recovery tech for Kubernetes-native workloads, for $150M

9月的一天，伊丽莎白·雷巴(Elizabeth Leiba)打开LinkedIn应用程序，看到了多样性顾问艾莎·约瑟夫(Aaisha Joseph)的一篇帖子，她在该平台上拥有近1.6万名粉丝。
这篇文章写道：“我需要#家公司停止派遣他们敬业的家庭黑人来‘对付那些他们认为失控的黑人’。”“这真的不好看--它实际上是一个非常#白人至上主义者和#种族主义者的形象。”
雷巴是佛罗里达州劳德代尔堡城市学院(City College)的教学设计经理，她想要的正是这份工作。这些天，当她拿出手机寻找喧闹的交谈、热闹的镜头和最新的茶时，她发现自己在点击LinkedIn，自从乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)被杀以来，LinkedIn已经成为一个蓬勃发展的黑人表达论坛。
46岁的莱巴说，“我上了Twitter，就会觉得无聊。”“然后我马上回到LinkedIn，因为它着火了。我现在甚至不用上任何其他社交媒体了。“。
对于长期以来被认为是最有礼貌，也可能是最乏味的主要社交网络来说，这是一个意想不到的发展。LinkedIn成立于2003年，是一个建立网络和发布简历的地方-本质上是一个白领专业人士的目录。几年前，LinkedIn添加了一个类似Facebook的新闻源，鼓励用户发布链接和更新，但它从来都不是一个活跃的空间。一个编辑团队帮助强化了一种最适合描述为公司的情绪。
2019年8月，LinkedIn的主编丹·罗斯(Dan Roth)告诉《纽约时报》：“你在LinkedIn上说话的方式和你在办公室里说话的方式是一样的。”“关于什么是可接受的，有一定的界限。”
两个令人震惊的事件改变了这一点。2020年初，大流行来袭，迫使数百万人在家工作，错过休息室闲聊的机会-推动LinkedIn成为发泄的地方。然后，弗洛伊德在5月份被警方拘留期间被杀，这让工人们陷入了困境。黑人的悲痛在美国企业界的虚拟饮水机上肆无忌惮地展示出来。
“我只是累了43年，”威斯康星州一所初中和高中的社交和情感学习主管Future Cain说。“在流感大流行期间，我用LinkedIn发布积极的东西，鼓舞人们，我决定不能再安静地坐在这里了。”
当抗议者走上街头要求警察改革时，莱巴和凯恩等人发现，LinkedIn是一个在类似他们的家乡的地方向高管阶层发表讲话的地方。黑人用户在该网站上呼吁工作场所的种族歧视，并分享他们在工作中被疏远的故事。
并不是所有的帖子都是认真的：帖子的大部分内容都很活跃-充满了迷因、黑人文化参考和语言华丽。今年夏天，雷巴分享了一段关于代码转换的视频，视频中一名黑人员工一边变身，一边与有色人种同事打招呼(“哦，嘿，黑皇后！”)。还有一个白色的(徒步旅行的空谈)。“我至少看了五十十一遍，”雷巴写道。
这些都是居住在隔间里的黑人工人通常在白人同事听不到的地方进行的谈话类型和说话方式。今年夏天，随着异常有魅力的帖子出现在我自己的帖子中，似乎很明显，黑人LinkedIn正在成为黑人Twitter的专业表亲--黑人Twitter是一个毫无歉意的数字空间，人们在这里揭露长期被忽视的不公正，并将他们的经历注入主流。
不太清楚的是，LinkedIn对这一发展有多满意，它将其内容版主置于火药味十足的位置，决定什么样的种族相关言论适合其7.06亿用户的虚拟工作场所。
以强硬语气发帖的黑人用户以及他们的一些盟友表示，他们觉得LinkedIn让他们保持了沉默-删除了他们的帖子，甚至冻结了他们的账户，因为他们违反了模糊的礼仪规则。
例如，约瑟夫在9月份写的“黑人之家”帖子从平台上消失了。住在布鲁克林的约瑟夫女士在浏览自己的页面时可以看到它，但其他用户看不到-这种做法被称为影子禁止。(后来，LinkedIn添加了一条红色的未署名注释，称该帖子因违反该网站的专业社区政策而被删除，这些政策要求用户“在每一次互动中都要有礼貌和尊重”。)。约瑟夫开始了一个新的话题：“让我大声点说，因为LinkedIn第一次想要删除我的帖子。”该公司也删除了这篇帖子，称其包括“骚扰、诽谤或诋毁他人”。
另一名用户特蕾莎·M·罗宾逊(Theresa M.Robinson)是休斯顿的一名企业培训顾问，她说LinkedIn删除了她写的一篇关于种族主义的帖子，然后在她投诉后恢复了这篇帖子。她说，她从未收到过解释。另外两人，凯恩和在德克萨斯州奥斯汀工作的麦迪逊·巴特勒(Madison Butler)也表示，LinkedIn限制了他们对种族的评论。
在公司没有明确沟通的情况下，这些用户只能猜测规则是什么-感觉公司不仅在规范他们的语气，而且扼杀了他们迫使美国企业界改变的机会。
LinkedIn发言人妮可·莱弗里奇(Nicole Leverich)在一封电子邮件中写道：“我们没有审查内容，也没有对我们的算法进行任何修改，以减少关于这些重要话题的内容的分发。”她在一次采访中补充说，LinkedIn正在引入一个新的流程，当用户的帖子被标记为违反平台规则时，通知用户，到9月底，一些人还没有逐步加入。
该公司承认，它在对一些用户采取行动时犯了错误，并恢复了经上诉发现没有违反其政策的内容。
LinkedIn的信任和安全部门负责人保罗·罗克韦尔(Paul Rockwell)说，“如果我们犯了错误，我们将承担责任。”“我们会非常清楚--这对我们来说是一个学习的机会。我们将继续在我们的旅程中使用它来变得越来越好。我们确实想要解决这件事。“。
几乎没有人认为LinkedIn应该看起来像Reddit或Twitter的狂野，这两家公司的DNA中都有一定程度的匿名性，甚至是无政府状态。LinkedIn的大部分价值-微软在2016年以260亿美元收购了它-与其专业精神和尊重他人的行为息息相关。用户必须共享他们的真实姓名和凭据，而且可以理解的是，他们现在或未来的雇主很可能会扫描他们发布的任何内容。
然而，对于企业界的黑人来说，像“专业”和“尊重”这样的词是危险信号。就像曾经被广泛认为是不专业的天然黑色发型一样，某些行为-太黑、说太黑或太多谈论黑人话题-长期以来在白人文化的公司里一直限制着晋升。
这就是LinkedIn在过去几个月里发生的变化。用一个专业术语来说，黑人在LinkedIn上被称为“黑-黑黑”(Blackity-Black Black on LinkedIn)。很多行为与黑色推特没有太大不同；用户在帖子中充斥着鼓掌表情符号，以强调每个音节，而GIF则颂扬文化试金石，比如伊萨·雷(Issa Rae)的“不安全”(Insecure)和乔丹·皮尔(Jordan Peele)的“滚出去”(Get Out)。不同的是，这一切都发生在一个反映商业世界的社交网络上-一个以白人为主的地方。
“这是一种解放。感觉是时候了，“约瑟夫说。“我们正在夺回被偷走的东西--这就是我们的声音。我专门用我们在其他空间相互交谈的方式与我的人交谈，而不考虑任何外部听众。不再需要扼杀那些一直在解放的东西。“。
黑人LinkedIn所做的部分工作是将黑人专业人士聚集在一起，在白人同事面前做真实的自己。对许多人来说，这是一种事关生存的解脱，可能会为黑人员工在实体工作场所重新开放后选择如何行为提供蓝图。
33岁的杰西卡·帕姆在密尔沃基附近的一家制造公司从事人力资源部的工作，她说，“那些隐藏和掩饰自己是谁，并与英国广播公司打交道的日子--我简直不能再回到那种日子了，”33岁的杰西卡·帕姆在密尔沃基附近的一家制造公司工作。“任何下一个得到我的公司都会得到全面的杰西卡。”
雷巴女士在9月9日发布了这篇帖子。17：“这意味着密码转换已经过时了。这意味着非洲人每天都在向你袭来。这意味着你会得到这些手镯和鲜艳的唇彩。“。
不可避免的是，并不是每个人都接受这种繁荣。关于黑人生命重要和种族正义的帖子往往会招致其他平台上同样的轻蔑，有时甚至是偏执的回应：例如，反驳“所有人的生命都很重要”，或者声称黑人对黑人的犯罪。但由于活动发生在LinkedIn上，这些评论通常会附上用户的头像、工作地点和整个工作历史。
39岁的小约翰·格雷厄姆(John Graham Jr.)是加州一家生物技术公司的数字营销员和策略师，他说，“你开始在评论中看到这些对黑人的关注绝对不满意的人，他们的名字和公司都被完全展示出来，对这一时刻毫无尊重。”“我发现这很能说明问题，人们会把自己的职业生涯置于危险之中，他们潜意识中的偏见会被充分展示出来。”
LinkedIn内部也一直在为如何回应“黑人生命也是命”(Black Lives Matter)运动而苦苦挣扎。今年6月，首席执行官瑞安·罗斯斯基(Ryan Roslansky)为一些员工在全公司员工会议上发表的“骇人听闻”的种族言论公开道歉。
LinkedIn的多样性、包容性和归属感负责人罗莎娜·杜鲁西(Rosanna Durruthy)在接受采访时表示，公司内部和外部都在就种族问题进行艰难的对话。
她表示，“我们真的开始非常始终如一地关注如何开始在外部解决这个问题，”在这个平台上。
在Black LinkedIn上呼声最高的人物之一是巴特勒，她是一家初创企业的人力资源顾问和副总裁。她自2018年以来一直在LinkedIn上发帖，今年发帖的频率和热情越来越高。她说，向资本说出真相的可能性，使得由此产生的几轮死亡威胁是值得的。
29岁的巴特勒说，“LinkedIn让你接触到有权势的首席执行官和风投，从而帮助改变他们的观点，以及他们对黑人员工和创始人的支持方式，这一点值得一提。”她指的是风险资本家。“打破美国企业界现状所必须进行的对话并没有发生在Instagram上。”
巴特勒拥有约4万名粉丝，她每天都在LinkedIn上发帖。她的风格是循规蹈矩，抨击公司规范，呼唤白人申诉者和巨魔；她倾向于在每封信件的结尾都贴上#isaidWhat Said，#That sthetea和#Black Girls Magic的标签。最近的一篇帖子指责了那些为黑人生命也是命(Black Lives Matter)运动欢呼，但没有正确对待员工的公司。
“你的组织中的黑人是否觉得他们很重要，或者他们觉得你用来增强你在市场上的‘Wokness’足迹的黑人股票照片。巴特勒女士最近写道：“如果你不能让”黑人生活“在你自己的屋檐下变得重要，就不要把”黑人生活也是重要“作为一种品牌战略。“别说了，好好想想吧。句号。“。
Black LinkedIn的其他明星针对的是特定的公司。例如，约瑟夫最近批评了富国银行(Wells Fargo)、DoorDash、微软(Microsoft)和谷歌(Google)。
对LinkedIn本身的批评也不在少数。用户对该公司的要求符合它在6月份为自己设定的标准，当时首席营销和公关官梅丽莎·塞尔彻(Melissa Selcher)在该平台上写了一封公开信。
她写道：“我们有责任利用我们的平台和资源，有意识地解决阻碍经济机会的系统性障碍。”“我们还认为，我们在放大黑人声音方面发挥了关键作用。”
同样是在6月，随着“黑人生命也是命”(Black Lives Matter)抗议活动在全国蔓延，LinkedIn重点推出了“要关注和放大的黑人声音”(Black Voices To Follow And Amplify)，这是一份精心策划的名单，包括首席执行官、媒体名人和其他有影响力的人，包括Netflix节目“酷儿眼”(Queer Eye)中的伯尼斯·金牧师(Rev.Bernices King)和卡拉莫·布朗(Kar。在大多数情况下，榜单上的成员发布的内容都是一般性的、激励性的和安全的。
约瑟夫和其他人在领英(LinkedIn)上表示，该组织包含了太多的权势人物，而没有足够的活动人士。“领英(LinkedIn)的塔米卡·马洛里(Tamika Mallorys)在那份名单上呢？”约瑟夫写道，她指的是2017年妇女大游行的一名联合创始人。
拉斯维加斯的人才招聘专家帕特里夏·S·加特林(Patricia S.Gatlin)写道：“黑人的声音不仅仅是企业高管的声音。”洛杉矶的招聘人员斯科特·泰勒(Scott Taylor)补充道：“此时此刻，所有黑人的声音都需要被听到。”“不仅仅是你们的分析师团队认为我们应该听取的那些人的意见。”
LinkedIn的女发言人莱弗里奇在电子邮件中说：“我们的选择考虑了很多因素，包括自称黑人的会员，来自不同行业的人，以及分享有趣观点的人。”我们不断增加新的声音，整理加入这个项目的请求。“。
今年9月，LinkedIn在自己的公司主页上向其1300万粉丝提出了一个问题：“在工作场所就种族和反种族主义进行对话的最好方式是什么？#ConversationsForChange。”
这些回应很快就变得酸溜溜的。华盛顿特区的沟通策略师Lenzy Ruffin写道：“LinkedIn，你可以通过在你的平台上将这些对话正常化来促进这一目标。”
“你竟然把这个贴出来，真是讽刺！”伦敦的多样性和包容性顾问阿比·亚当森(Abi Adamson)写道。“请停止围绕种族主义审查黑人内容。像我这样的人在强调种族主义或如何成为反种族主义者时，我们的订婚经历了天文数字的下降。帮助放大我们的声音，停止让我们沉默。“。
西雅图的一名研究生Sabrina McClimate ans要求该平台“停止‘意外地’在你的平台上消失黑人女性的帖子，当她们谈论种族和反种族主义时。”
在芝加哥一家软件公司工作的菲尔·莫莱(Phil Molé)补充说：“我见过在这个平台上骚扰黑人女性的个人保留了自己的账户，而那些公开反对种族主义和偏见的人的账户被暂停。”“现在是彻底检讨问题处理方式的时候了。”
LinkedIn没有回应这些评论。伦敦的咨询师菲利普·米克斯(Philip Mix)在一天半后添加了这条帖子，当时有344条评论，他说他已经“看了三遍，以确保我没有弄错”。根据他的统计，LinkedIn已经回复了五个用户-四次说“谢谢你的分享”，一次说“放得好”。
米克斯的结论是：“我不确定LinkedIn的这个糟糕透顶的节目让我更震惊还是更沮丧。”
note_en:
One day in September, Elizabeth Leiba opened the LinkedIn app and saw a post by Aaisha Joseph, a diversity consultant with nearly 16,000 followers on the platform.
 “Ima need #companies to stop sending their dedicated House Negros to ‘deal with the Blacks’ they deem out of control,” read the item. “It’s really not a good look — it’s actually a very #whitesupremacist and #racist one.”
 The post was exactly the sort of thing Ms. Leiba, an instructional design manager at City College in Fort Lauderdale, Fla., was looking for. These days, when she pulls out her phone in search of boisterous conversation, hot takes and the latest tea, she finds herself tapping LinkedIn, which since  the killing of George Floyd has become a thriving forum for Black expression.
 “I go onto Twitter and I get bored,” Ms. Leiba, 46, said. “Then I go right back to LinkedIn because it’s on fire. I don’t even have to go on any other social media now.”
 It’s an unexpected development for what has long been the most polite and perhaps the dullest of the major social networks. LinkedIn was founded in 2003 as a place to network and post résumés — essentially, a directory of white-collar professionals. A few years ago, LinkedIn added a Facebook-like news feed that encouraged users to post links and updates, but it has never been a rollicking space. A team of editors helped enforce a mood best described as corporate.
 “You talk on LinkedIn the same way you talk in the office,” Dan Roth, LinkedIn’s editor in chief,  told The New York Times in August 2019. “There are certain boundaries around what is acceptable.”
 Two staggering events have changed that. In early 2020, the pandemic hit, forcing millions to work from home and miss out on break-room chitchat — boosting LinkedIn as a place to vent. Then, the killing of Mr. Floyd in police custody in May put workers over the edge. Black grief went on display, uninhibited, at corporate America’s virtual water cooler.
 “I was just 43 years tired,” said Future Cain, a social and emotional learning director at a middle and high school in Wisconsin. “I was using LinkedIn to post positive things and uplift people during the pandemic, and I decided I can’t sit here quietly anymore.”
 As protesters took to the streets to demand police reform, Ms. Leiba and Ms. Cain were among those who discovered that LinkedIn was a place to speak to the executive class on something like their home turf. Black users have taken to the site to call out racial discrimination in the workplace and share their stories of  alienation on the job.
 Not that it’s all serious: Much of the posting is exuberant — full of memes, Black cultural references and linguistic panache. This summer, Ms. Leiba shared a video about  code-switching, in which a Black employee transforms while greeting colleagues of color (“Oh,  hey, Black queen!”) and a white one (empty-headed hiking talk). “I’ve watched it at least fifty eleven times,” Ms. Leiba wrote.
 These are the kinds of conversations, and ways of speaking, that cubicle-dwelling Black workers have typically held out of earshot of their white colleagues. As unusually charismatic posts appeared in my own feed this summer, it seemed clear that Black LinkedIn was emerging as a professional cousin to  Black Twitter — the unapologetically Black digital space where people expose long-ignored injustices and pump their experience into the mainstream.
 What’s less clear is how comfortable LinkedIn is with the development, having placed its content moderators in the incendiary position of determining what manner of race-related speech is appropriate for its virtual workplace of 706 million users.
  Black users who post in forceful tones, and some of their allies, say they feel LinkedIn has silenced them — erasing their posts and even freezing their accounts for violating vague rules of decorum.
 For example, the “House Negros” post that Ms. Joseph wrote in September vanished from the platform. Ms. Joseph, who lives in Brooklyn, was able to see it when she viewed her own page, but no other users could — a practice known as  shadow banning. (Later, LinkedIn added an unsigned note in red, saying the post had been removed for violating the site’s  Professional Community Policies, which instruct users to “be civil and respectful in every single interaction.”) Ms. Joseph began a new item: “Let me say it louder since LinkedIn wanted to delete my post the first time.” The company removed that post, too, saying it included “harassment, defamation or disparagement of others.”
 Another user, Theresa M. Robinson, a corporate training consultant in Houston, said LinkedIn had  deleted a post she wrote about racism, then reinstated it after she complained. She said she had never received an explanation. Two others, Ms. Cain and Madison Butler, who works in Austin, Texas, also said LinkedIn had restricted their commentary on race.
 In the absence of clear communication from the company, these users are left guessing as to what the rules are — and feeling that the company is not just policing their tone but stifling their opportunity to force change in corporate America.
 Nicole Leverich, a LinkedIn spokeswoman, wrote in an email: “We are not censoring content and have not made any changes to our algorithm to reduce the distribution of content about these important topics.” She added in an interview that LinkedIn was introducing a new process for notifying users when their posts were flagged for violating platform rules, and that some people hadn’t been phased in by the end of September.
 The company acknowledged that it had erred in taking action against some users and restored content that was found, on appeal, not to violate its policies.
 “If we make a mistake, we will own it,” said Paul Rockwell, the head of LinkedIn’s trust and safety division. “We will be very clear — this is a learning opportunity for us. We’re going to continue to use that in our journey to get better and better. And we do want to nail this thing.”
  Few people think LinkedIn should look anything like the wilds of Reddit or Twitter, which have a certain amount of anonymity and even anarchy built into their DNA. Much of LinkedIn’s value — Microsoft acquired it in 2016 for  $26 billion — is tied to its sense of professionalism and respectful conduct. Users must share their real names and credentials, and it’s understood that their current or prospective employers might well scan anything they post.
 For Black people in the corporate realm, however, words like “professional” and “respectful” are red flags. Like the natural Black hairstyles that were once widely considered unprofessional, certain behaviors — being too Black, speaking too Black or talking too much about Black topics — have long limited advancement in companies with white cultures.
 That’s what has changed on LinkedIn in the last few months. Black people are being, to use a technical term,  Blackity-Black Black on LinkedIn. Much of the behavior is not so different from Black Twitter; users pepper their posts with clap emojis to emphasize every syllable, and GIFs celebrate cultural touchstones like Issa Rae’s “Insecure” and Jordan Peele’s “Get Out.” The difference is that it is all happening on a social network that mirrors the business world — a place that is predominantly white.
 “It is liberating. It feels like it’s about time,” Ms. Joseph said. “We are taking back what was stolen from us — and that’s our voice. I’m talking specifically to my people in the way that we talk to each other in other spaces, and without regard for any outside audience. No longer having to stifle that has been freeing.”
 Part of what Black LinkedIn has done is brought together Black professionals to be their authentic selves in front of their white colleagues. For many, it has been an existential relief, and may provide a blueprint for how Black employees choose to conduct themselves once the physical workplace reopens.
 “The days of hiding and masking who you are and dealing with the BS — I just can’t even go back to that,” said  Jessica Pharm, 33, who works in human resources at a manufacturing firm near Milwaukee. “Any company that gets me next is getting the full-on Jessica.”
 Ms. Leiba  posted on Sept. 17: “It means code-switching is OUT. It means the AFRO is coming at you on a daily basis. It means you’re getting these bangle earrings and the poppin’ lip gloss.”
 Inevitably, not everyone accepts this kind of exuberance. Posts about Black Lives Matter and racial justice often attract the same kind of dismissive, and sometimes bigoted, responses found on other platforms: rejoinders that “all lives matter,” for instance, or claims about Black-on-Black crime. But because the activity takes place on LinkedIn, these comments typically come with the user’s headshot, place of employment and entire work history attached.
 “You start to see these people who are absolutely not OK with this focus on Blackness popping up in commentary, with their name and their company fully on display, giving zero deference to the moment,” said John Graham Jr., 39, a digital marketer and strategist at a California biotechnology company. “I find it telling that people would put their careers in jeopardy and their unconscious biases on full display.”
 LinkedIn has also struggled internally with how to respond to the Black Lives Matter movement. In June, the chief executive, Ryan Roslansky,  publicly apologized for “appalling” racial  comments some employees had made at a companywide staff meeting.
 Rosanna Durruthy, LinkedIn’s head of diversity, inclusion and belonging, said in an interview that the company was engaging in hard conversations about race, both inside the company and out.
 “We’re really beginning to focus very consistently on how we begin to address this externally” on the platform, she said.
  One of the most vociferous presences on Black LinkedIn is  Ms. Butler, a human resources consultant and vice president at a start-up. She has posted on LinkedIn since 2018 and with increasing frequency and fervor this year. The potential to speak truth to capital, she said, makes the resulting rounds of death threats worth it.
 “There is something to be said about the access LinkedIn gives you to powerful C.E.O.s and V.C.s to help change their outlook and how they support Black employees and founders,” said Ms. Butler, 29, referring to venture capitalists. “The conversation that has to happen in order to break down the status quo in corporate America isn’t happening on Instagram.”
 Ms. Butler, who has about 40,000 followers, posts on LinkedIn daily. Her style is to be prescriptive, assail corporate norms and call out whitesplainers and trolls; she tends to close each missive with the hashtags #isaidwhatisaid, #thatsthetea and #blackgirlmagic. One recent post scolded companies that make a show of cheering on the Black Lives Matter movement but haven’t done right by their employees.
 “Do the Black people in your organization feel like they matter, or do they feel like the Black stock photos you used to enhance your ‘wokeness’ footprint in the marketplace. If you can’t make the Black lives under your own roof matter, do not use Black Lives Matter as a brand strategy,” Ms. Butler wrote recently. “Don’t talk about it, be about it. Period.”
 Other stars of Black LinkedIn target specific companies. Ms. Joseph, for example, has recently called out Wells Fargo, DoorDash, Microsoft and Google.
 There has also been no shortage of criticism of LinkedIn itself. Users are holding the company to a standard it set for itself in June, when Melissa Selcher, the chief marketing and communications officer, wrote an  open letter on the platform.
 “We have a responsibility to use our platform and resources to intentionally address the systemic barriers to economic opportunity,” she wrote. “We also believe we play a critical role in amplifying Black voices.”
 Also in June, with Black Lives Matter protests spreading across the country, LinkedIn highlighted “Black Voices to Follow and Amplify,” a curated list of chief executives, media personalities and other influencers, including the Rev. Bernice King and Karamo Brown from the Netflix show “Queer Eye.” For the most part, members of the list post content that is general, motivational and safe.
 Ms. Joseph and  others took to LinkedIn to say the group contained too many establishment names and  not enough activists. “Where are the Tamika Mallorys of LinkedIn on that list?”  Ms. Joseph wrote, referring to a co-founder of the  2017 Women’s March.
 “Black voices aren’t just corporate C-Suite ones,” wrote Patricia S. Gatlin, a talent sourcing specialist in Las Vegas. “All Black voices need to be heard in this moment,” added Scott Taylor, a recruiter in Los Angeles. “Not just the ones your team of analysts think we should hear from.”
 Ms. Leverich, the LinkedIn spokeswoman, said by email: “We use a number of factors in our selection, including members who have self-identified as Black, people from a variety of industries and with an interesting perspective to share. We’re constantly adding new voices and sorting through requests to join this program.”
 In September, LinkedIn used its own company page to pose a  question to its 13 million followers: “What are the best ways to normalize having conversations about race and anti-racism in the workplace? #ConversationsForChange”
 The responses quickly turned sour. “LinkedIn, you can facilitate that objective by normalizing those conversations on your platform,” wrote  Lenzy Ruffin, a communications strategist in Washington, D.C.
 “The irony that you should post this!” wrote  Abi Adamson, a diversity and inclusion consultant in London. “Kindly stop censoring Black content around racism. People like me have had our engagement go down astronomically when highlighting racism or how to be anti racist. Help amplify our voices and stop silencing us.”
 Sabrina McClimans, a graduate student in Seattle, asked the platform to “stop ‘accidentally’ disappearing the posts of Black women on your platform when they talk about race and anti-racism.”
 “I have seen cases in which individuals who harass Black women on this platform have maintained their accounts while those who speak out against racism and prejudice have had accounts suspended,” added  Phil Molé, who works at a software company in Chicago. “It’s time for a thorough review of the way the issues are handled.”
 LinkedIn did not respond to those comments. Philip Mix, a consultant in London, added to the thread after a day and a half, when there were 344 comments, saying he had gone through them “three times to make sure I wasn’t mistaken.” By  his  count, LinkedIn had replied to five users — four times to say “Thank you for sharing” and once with “Nicely put.”
 Mr. Mix concluded: “Not sure if I’m more shocked or depressed by this miserably inadequate show from LinkedIn.”
title: 自从大流行和乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)被杀以来，黑人领英(Black LinkedIn)正成为黑人推特(Black Twitter)的专业类比，人们对节制的看法出现了分歧
title_en: Black LinkedIn is emerging as a professional analog to Black Twitter since the pandemic and George Floyd's killing, with disagreements about moderation arising

note:
为程序室开发远程医疗软件的医疗技术公司avail Medsystems在B系列融资中筹集了1亿美元。
D1 Capital Partners在8VC和现有投资者的参与下领导了这轮融资。这笔新资金使这家总部位于帕洛阿尔托的公司自2018年成立以来总共获得了1.25亿美元的资金。根据Crunchbase的数据，这包括2500万美元的首轮和首轮A-1融资。
Avail吹捧拥有“唯一的端到端系统，可以帮助手术室中的外科团队与远程医疗专家协作进行现场程序和临床培训。”
AVail Medsystems首席执行官丹尼尔·霍金斯(Daniel Hawkins)在一份书面声明中表示，此前，病例协作依赖于面对面的沟通，这是昂贵、耗时和有风险的，因为这会增加感染的可能性。
他补充说：“我们专门设计的技术消除了这些障碍，使医疗协作、信息共享和培训能够独立于地点。”虽然我们的旅程早在新冠肺炎之前就开始了，但大流行已经让这些问题成为聚光灯下的焦点，并加速了远程出现在手术室的需要。
新的资金将用于增加更多的医学专家到其网络，增加新的临床专科，并进一步渗透当前领域，以及产品开发工作。
note_en:
Medical technology company  Avail Medsystems, which develops telemedicine software for the procedure room, raised $100 million in Series B funding.
  D1 Capital Partners led the round with participation from  8VC and existing investors. The new funding gives the Palo Alto-based company a total of $125 million in funding since it was started in 2018. This includes a $25 million in Series A and Series A-1 funding, according to Crunchbase data.
 Avail touts having “the only end-to-end system that helps surgical teams in operating rooms collaborate with remote medical experts for live procedures and clinical training.”
 Previously, case collaboration depended on in-person communication, which was costly, time-consuming and risky in terms of increasing the possibility of infection,  Daniel Hawkins, Avail Medsystems CEO, said in a written statement.
 “Our purpose-built technology removes these barriers, enabling medical collaboration, information sharing and training independent of location,” he added. “While our journey began long before COVID-19, the pandemic has shone a spotlight on these issues and accelerated the need for remote presence in the operating room.”
 The new funding will be used to add more medical experts to its network, add new clinical specialties and further penetrate current areas, as well as work on product development.
title: Avail Medsystems开发远程医疗软件，使手术室的外科团队能够与远程医疗专家协作，该公司筹集了1亿美元的B系列资金
title_en: Avail Medsystems, which develops telemedicine software to let surgical teams in operating rooms collaborate with remote medical experts, raises $100M Series B

note:
欢迎来到来源笔记，这是一个关于互联网知识生态系统的未来时态专栏。
当最高法院法官鲁思·巴德·金斯伯格(Ruth Bader Ginsburg)在9月份去世时，她在维基百科上的文章一举登上了“前25名报告”(Top 25 Report)的榜首，这是一份每周一次的英文维基百科最受欢迎文章排行榜。那一周，金斯伯格的文章获得了超过400万的页面浏览量。但在2020年，已故名人的英文维基百科页面上流量最大的不是“臭名昭著的RBG”，而是一位在自己国家以外相对默默无闻的印度电影明星。
那就是34岁的宝莱坞演员苏珊特·辛格·拉吉普特(Sushant Singh Rajput)，他于6月14日上吊身亡，显然是自杀身亡。在SSR去世的那一周，他的页面浏览量达到了1150万次。据编制这份前25名报告的维基百科志愿者表示，只有5篇文章的每周浏览量超过1000万大关-普林斯、大卫·鲍伊、科比·布莱恩特和斯蒂芬·霍金去世后的文章，以及唐纳德·特朗普在2016年大选后的文章。
自6月中旬以来，SSR的维基百科传记页面和关于他死亡的单独文章已经成为一个被广泛传播的互联网战场。编辑们激烈争论维基百科是否应该反映关于SSR死因的阴谋论，以及互联网百科全书是否正确地描述了演员的身高。(很大程度上取决于他的身高是否低于6英尺。)。在事实似乎越来越难达成一致的时候，维基百科对SSR之死的回应表明，即使是善意地尝试记录可靠的信息，也可能无意中助长阴谋论者。
SSR被视为一个局外人，尽管没有乔普拉(Chopra)或卡普尔(Kapoor)这样著名的宝莱坞姓氏，但他奇迹般地打入了印度电影制作的小圈子世界。在2009年至2011年主演肥皂剧帕维特拉·里什塔之后，他的电影处女作是在开普车！2013年。他在2016年的传记电影M.S.Dhoni：The Untaled Story中扮演了一名板球队长，为此他获得了电影奖(FilmFlow Award)的提名，有时被称为印地语电影业的奥斯卡奖(Academy Awards)。所有人都说，SSR是一颗冉冉升起的明星。
孟买警方7月份的报告得出结论，SSR的死亡是窒息自杀。但在公众的强烈抗议以及印度总理莫迪所在的保守派政党印度人民党政界人士的压力下，印度最高法院在8月裁定，中央调查局将自行进行调查。
印度媒体对SSR的死亡进行了多少报道都不为过，包括展示他身体的照片。今年夏天，印度电视网对这位宝莱坞演员之死的关注超过了新冠肺炎。正如米凯拉·斯通·克罗斯(Michaela Stone Cross)为“华尔街日报”所写的那样，“被减少的新闻自由和更多的假新闻掏空的媒体正在发布它能获得的任何信息，无论是真的还是假的。”印度各地的屏幕上都能看到拉吉普特的脸，翻白眼，撕裂的鲜活，还有三个粉丝以他的名字自杀。“。保守派电视主持人指责SSR的女友Rhea Chakraborty是一个“操控欲强”的女人，她“施展黑魔法”，“逼迫苏珊特自杀”。有人打电话给#ArrestRheaNow。
数百万被称为“SSRians”的人正在使用社交媒体进行他们自己的研究，挑战官方报告，并公布他们自己版本的真相。#Justicefor Sushant阵营的人抛出了无数的阴谋论，包括SSR是被宝莱坞黑手党谋杀的，或者他的女朋友Chakraborty用药物毒死了他，以抑制他的情绪。
当然，争议已经蔓延到维基百科，在那些认为发生了可疑事情的人和那些致力于维基百科精神的人之间制造了一场斗争。正如维基百科编辑内德福萨在他们的用户页面上详细叙述的那样，事情一开始就让人难以置信，因为它被编辑得如此之快，以反映他已经去世了。我以前写过关于死亡的过程-更新维基百科页面以反映人们的死亡-以及它如何没有听起来那么可怕。但在SSR的情况下，似乎一些旁观者已经准备好怀疑发生了什么不正当的事情。“他的死因在6月14日上午8点59分更新了@维基百科。这怎么可能？“一位用户在推特上写道，他很担心，因为SSR在上午11：30之间死亡。下午1：30。当地时间。维基百科怎么能在SSR去世前几个小时更新他的死讯呢？但是维基百科使用协调世界时，而不是印度标准时间。IST等于UTC+05：30。考虑到世界协调时的调整，维基百科编辑的时间与印地语电视台发布这一消息的时刻完全一致。然而，这个简单的误解导致阴谋家们得出结论，用内德福萨的话说，“维基百科在偷偷地与谋杀SSR的人密谋。”
批评家还指责维基百科是警方在SSR死后萎缩的同谋。黑暗的理论是这样的：风扇马达和他的床之间的距离是5英尺11“，但SSR在无数次采访中描述他的身高为6英尺(183厘米)，这让一些人得出结论，他不可能自杀。在他死后，页面被更新为SSR是5英尺10英寸“。“警方谎报了他的身高，我们一发现苏珊不可能吊在粉丝…上，维基百科就改变了他的身高。”7月份，一名用户在推特上写道。
这导致了一系列针对@Wikipedia账户的推文，要求修改SSR的高度。但将责任归咎于维基百科作为一个实体表明了对该网站运作方式的根本误解，而且这一错误肯定不仅仅局限于印度。世界各地的维基百科编辑创建并拥有互联网百科全书的内容，而不是总部设在旧金山的非营利性维基百科基金会。
取而代之的是，志愿编辑社区开始为SSR的高度而争论不休。首先，列出SSR高度为5‘10“的信息被编辑删除，因为添加的信息没有来源。然后有人把它放回去了。这时，一名志愿者管理员介入，根据传记文章通常不包括身高的政策，永久删除了SSR的身高，除非这个人因为身高而特别引人注目。(这就是为什么彼得·丁拉奇的主页上列出了他的身高，但在“权力的游戏”中扮演他虚构的兄弟姐妹的两名演员的身高没有列出。)。不过，值得考虑的是，哪种类型的震源足够可靠，可以添加有关SSR高度的信息。关于SSR高度的信息需要来自传统的新闻出版物，如“印度快报”或“印度教徒报”吗？
除了身高问题，现在已经有几次尝试将SSR维基百科页面上的死因从“上吊自杀”改为“因上吊而窒息，调查中的态度”或更直接的术语“谋杀”。当我联系到活跃在SSR页面上的维基百科编辑时，他们强调了在维基百科上引用可靠消息来源的重要性，而不是引用小报。“由于(中央调查局)拒绝公布(正在进行的)调查的任何细节，没有可靠的消息来源报告了除自杀以外的任何死因，”这位化名TA解释道。他是印度卡纳塔克邦20多岁的维基百科一名多产的编辑。
维基百科上关于SSR和他的死亡的文章已经被锁定，并在不久的将来受到延长确认的保护。这意味着，只有活跃了30天并至少有500次编辑的维基百科编辑才能直接编辑这两个SSR页面。相比之下，露丝·巴德·金斯伯格(Ruth Bader Ginsburg)的页面是半保护的，以防止匿名IP编辑，当大量破坏来自未注册用户时，这是一种常见的措施，但它没有这么高的30/500限制。将SSR内容限制给更有经验的投稿人的理由是，如果没有保护，有生产力的编辑将被不间断的破坏性编辑完全淹没，以至于他们根本跟不上。
这种保护的一个副作用是，大部分讨论都转移到了每篇文章后面的谈话页面，在那里，数百名热情的、大多是新手的用户加入进来，倡导改变Deat的原因。
“看到人们淡化他可能的抑郁，我真的很恼火，”Slate文案编辑、宝莱坞影迷尼蒂什·帕瓦(Niish Pahwa)说。“现在在印度很难成为任何杰出的人物，特别是在这个两极分化的时代，(考虑到)身处世界上最多产的电影业的压力。”Pahwa指出，宝莱坞还有其他著名的悲剧死亡案例，比如传说中的20世纪50年代和60年代的演员兼导演古鲁·达特(Guru Dut)，他被认为患有阵发性抑郁症，在将酒精和安眠药混合后死亡，这被认为是他的第三次自杀未遂。
周六，协助CBI调查SSR的医疗队的一名代表宣布，他们没有发现挣扎的证据，并得出结论，他的死亡是自杀-重申了孟买警方的调查结果。但是像TA这样的印度维基百科编辑预计CBI的最终声明不会有太大的不同。TA写道：“人们的关注可能会消退。”“但我认为这不会改变那些已经得出自己结论的人的想法.”与此同时，公众似乎有一种根深蒂固的报复欲望。SSR的女友查克拉博蒂(Chakraborty)于9月份因涉嫌向这位已故演员提供少量大麻而被捕。尽管大麻在印度盛行，但政府对大麻的销售和消费实施了严厉的惩罚和监禁判决，查克拉博蒂的被捕被广泛视为右翼人民党政客的杰作，他们希望被视为打击犯罪的强硬分子。
令人遗憾的是，SSR的死亡已经成为一个政治楔子问题。这也是为什么维基百科的中立政策如此重要的案例研究。互联网百科全书有别于其他网络资源，因为它努力只收录来自可靠新闻来源的信息。这就是这个项目的价值所在：坚持自己乏味的过程，即使这意味着百科全书版本没有小报那么戏剧性。即使这是一种更常见的悲伤。
如果你正在处理自杀的想法，你可以联系到帮助，通过电话拨打美国国家自杀热线电话：1-800-273-8255，或者通过短信拨打美国危机短信热线741-741(在几乎所有的运营商上，这些短信都是免费的)。
未来时态是Slate、New America和亚利桑那州立大学的合作项目，研究新兴技术、公共政策和社会。
note_en:
Welcome to   Source Notes , a Future Tense column about the internet’s knowledge ecosystem.
  When Supreme Court Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg died in September, her Wikipedia article rocketed to the top of the “Top 25 Report,” a weekly list of the most popular articles on English Wikipedia. That week, Ginsburg’s article received  more than 4 million page views. But the most-trafficked English Wikipedia page for a deceased celebrity in 2020 was  not that of the “Notorious RBG,” but rather an Indian movie star who was relatively unknown outside his own country.
    That would be  Sushant Singh Rajput, a 34-year-old Bollywood actor who died of apparent suicide by hanging on June 14. The week SSR—as he was and is known to fans—died, his page received  11.5 million page views. According to the Wikipedia volunteers who compile the Top 25 report, only five other articles have ever seen weekly page views pass the 10 million mark—those of Prince, David Bowie, Kobe Bryant, and Stephen Hawking after their deaths, and Donald Trump’s after the 2016 election.
      Since mid-June, SSR’s biographical Wikipedia page and the  separate article about his death have become a widely trafficked internet battleground. Editors have fiercely disputed whether Wikipedia should reflect conspiracy theories about SSR’s cause of death and if the internet encyclopedia correctly states the actor’s height. (There’s a lot riding on whether he was shorter than 6 feet.) In a time when it seems increasingly hard to agree upon the facts, Wikipedia’s response to SSR’s death shows how even good-faith attempts to document reliable information may inadvertently fuel the conspiracy theorists.
    SSR was seen as an outsider, someone who had miraculously broken into the cliquey world of Indian filmmaking despite not having a famous Bollywood surname like  Chopra or  Kapoor. After starring in the soap opera   Pavitra Rishta from 2009 to 2011, he made his film debut in   Kai Po Che! in 2013. He played a cricket captain in the 2016 biopic  M.S. Dhoni: The Untold Story, for which he was nominated for a Filmfare Award, sometimes referred to as the Hindi film industry’s equivalent of the Academy Awards. By all accounts, SSR was a star on the rise.
    The Mumbai Police’s report in July concluded that SSR’s death was suicide by asphyxiation. But after public outcry and pressure from politicians from the BJP, Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s conservative political party, India’s Supreme Court ruled in August that the Central Bureau of Investigation would conduct its own investigation.
    It’s hard to overstate how much Indian media has covered SSR’s death, including showing pictures of his dead body. There were weeks this summer when TV networks in India gave the Bollywood actor’s death more attention than COVID-19. As Michaela Stone Cross wrote for  the Juggernaut, “The media—hollowed out by decreasing press freedom and more fake news—was releasing any information it could get its hands on, real or fake. Rajput’s face, eyes rolling, lacerations vivid, could be seen on screens all across India, and three fans killed themselves in his name.” Conservative TV hosts accused  Rhea Chakraborty, SSR’s girlfriend, of being a “manipulative” woman who “performed black magic” and “ drove Sushant to suicide.” There were calls to  #ArrestRheaNow.
    Millions of people, dubbed “SSRians,” are using social media to conduct their own research, challenge the official reports, and publicize their own version of the truth. People in the  #JusticeforSushant camp have tossed out numerous conspiracy theories, including that SSR was murdered by the Bollywood mafia or that his girlfriend Chakraborty was poisoning him with  medication to depress his mood.
      Naturally, the controversy has spilled over to Wikipedia, creating a battle between those who think something suspicious happened and those committed to Wikipedia’s ethos. As the Wikipedia editor NedFausa  recounts in detail on their user page, it started with disbelief about how quickly it was edited to reflect that he had died. I’ve written before about  the process of deaditing—updating Wikipedia pages to reflect people’s deaths—and how it’s not quite as macabre as it sounds. But in the case of SSR, it appears some onlookers were primed to suspect something underhanded was going on. “The cause of his death updated @Wikipedia at 8:59 A.M. on 14 th June. How is this possible”  tweeted one user, who was concerned because SSR died between 11:30 a.m. and 1:30 p.m. local time. How could Wikipedia be updated with information about SSR’s death a few hours before he died? But Wikipedia uses Coordinated Universal Time as opposed to Indian Standard Time. IST is equal to UTC +05:30. Accounting for the adjustment to UTC, the timing of the Wikipedia-editing corresponded perfectly with the moment that Hindi-language TV stations broke the news. Nevertheless, this simple misunderstanding led the conspiracists to conclude, in the words of NedFausa, that “Wikipedia was furtively scheming with those who murdered SSR.”
    Critics have also accused Wikipedia of being the police’s co-conspirator in SSR’s posthumous shrinking. The  dark theory went like this: The distance between the fan motor and his bed was 5’11”, but SSR had described his height as  6 feet (183 centimeters) in numerous interviews, leaving some to conclude that it was impossible he had killed himself. After his death, the page was updated to say that  SSR was 5’ 10”. “The police are lying about his height, and wikipedia changed his height as soon as we caught onto the fact that its impossible for Sushant to have hung from the fan …”  tweeted one user in July.
  This led to a flurry of tweets directed to the @Wikipedia account demanding that SSR’s height be modified. But casting the blame on Wikipedia as an entity suggests a fundamental misunderstanding about how the site works, and it’s a mistake that’s certainly not limited to India. Wikipedia editors around the world create and own the content of the internet encyclopedia,  not the nonprofit Wikimedia Foundation based in San Francisco.
    Instead, the community of volunteer editors began tussling over SSR’s height. First the information listing SSR’s height as 5’ 10” was removed by an editor because there was no source for the addition. Then someone put it back in. That’s when  a volunteer administrator stepped in and removed SSR’s height permanently based on the policy that height is not typically included in a biographical article unless the person is specifically notable because of their height. (That’s why  Peter Dinklage’s page lists his height, but the heights of the two actors who played his fictional siblings on  Game of Thrones are not listed.) Then again, it’s worth considering what type of source  would be considered reliable enough for adding information about SSR’s height. Would information about SSR’s height need to come from a traditional news publication like the Indian Express or the Hindu?
        Besides the height issue, there have now been several attempts to change the cause of death on SSR’s Wikipedia page from “suicide by hanging” to “asphyxia due to hanging, manner under investigation” or the more direct term “murder.” When I reached out to Wikipedia editors who were active on the SSR pages, they emphasized the importance of citing trustworthy sources on Wikipedia rather than the tabloids. “Since the [Central Bureau of Investigation] has declined to publicise any details of the [ongoing] investigation, no reliable source has reported anything other than suicide as the cause of death,” explained the pseudonymous TA, a prolific twentysomething Wikipedia editor based in the Indian state of Karnataka.
  The Wikipedia articles about SSR and his death have been locked with  extended-confirmed protection for the near future. This means that only Wikipedia editors who have been active for 30 days and have at least 500 edits will be permitted to directly edit the two SSR pages. For comparison, Ruth Bader Ginsburg’s page is semi-protected to prevent  anonymous IP editing, a common measure when a significant amount of vandalism comes from unregistered users, but it doesn’t have this higher degree of 30/500 restriction. The argument for limiting the SSR content to more experienced contributors is that, without the protection, the productive editors would be so completely overwhelmed by the nonstop disruptive edits that they simply couldn’t keep up.
      A side effect of this protection is that much of the discussion has been moved to the  talk page that sits behind each article, where  hundreds of passionate, mostly newbie users have chimed in to advocate for changing the cause of death from suicide to murder. From TA’s perspective, the vitriol reflects cultural challenges in India when it comes to recognizing mental health issues.  TA told me via email that depression in India was sometimes perceived as a character flaw or an excuse for bad behavior. He added that he thought failure to recognize mental illness in India was one factor contributing to the conspiracy theories.
  Many reports suggest that the late actor did, in fact, suffer from significant mental health challenges.  The Mumbai Police report indicated that the late actor searched on Google for the terms “painless death,” “schizophrenia,” and “bipolar disorder” a few hours before he died. On Aug. 1, SSR’s therapist shared that the actor suffered from bipolar disorder,  explaining in an interview that the media’s “trivialization of mental health” was one reason that she felt compelled to come forward. (The therapist has since been sued by SSR’s family for professional misconduct and breaching doctor-patient confidentiality.)
        “It really rubs me the wrong way to see people downplaying his possible depression,” said  Nitish Pahwa, a Slate copy editor and Bollywood film buff. “It’s a very hard time to be any prominent figure in India right now, especially in this age of hyperpolarization and [given the] pressures of being in the most prolific film industry in the world.” Pahwa noted that there were other prominent examples of tragic Bollywood deaths such as the legendary 1950s and ’60s actor and director  Guru Dutt, who was known to  suffer from bouts of depression and died after mixing alcohol and sleeping pills in what is thought to be his third suicide attempt.
    On Saturday, a representative for the medical team assisting with the CBI’s investigation into SSR announced that they had found no evidence of struggle and concluded that his death was a suicide—reiterating the findings of the Mumbai Police. But Indian Wikipedia editors like TA don’t expect the CBI’s final announcement to make much of a difference. “The attention will probably die down,” TA wrote. “But I don’t think it is going to change the minds of people who have already come to their own conclusions.” Meanwhile, there appears to be a deep-seated public desire for revenge. SSR’s girlfriend, Chakraborty, was  arrested in September for allegedly supplying small amounts of marijuana to the late actor. Although cannabis is prevalent in India, the government has enacted harsh penalties and prison sentences for its sale and consumption, and Chakraborty’s arrest was widely seen as the handiwork of right-wing BJP politicians who want to be seen as tough on crime.
      Regrettably, SSR’s death has become a political wedge issue. It’s also a case study in why Wikipedia’s  neutrality policy is so important. The internet encyclopedia distinguishes itself from other web resources because it strives only to include information from reliable journalistic sources. That’s the value of the project: sticking to its own boring processes even if it means the encyclopedia version is less dramatic than the tabloids. Even if it’s  a more common type of sad.
  If you are dealing with suicidal thoughts, you can reach out for help, by phone to the   National Suicide Hotline  at 1-800-273-8255, or over text message to the   Crisis Text Line  at 741-741 (on almost all carriers, these texts are free).
  Future Tense is a partnership of  Slate,  New America, and  Arizona State University that examines emerging technologies, public policy, and society.
title: 一位宝莱坞明星的维基百科页面已经成为互联网战场，因为编辑们对它是否应该反映关于他死因的阴谋论存在争议。
title_en: The Wikipedia page of a Bollywood star has become an internet battleground as editors dispute if it should reflect conspiracy theories about his cause of death

note:
政治邮递员伪装成进步选民指南，支持由优步和Lyft支持的22号提案，出现在南加州选民的邮箱里。
其中一封邮件上的小字说，它是由“感受伯尔尼，进步选民指南”准备的，而这并不是一个真正的组织。关注女性选民指南理事会(Council Of Concerned Women Voters Guide)和我们的声音“拉丁裔选民指南”(Voice Our Voter Guide)都不是，后者的邮件代言人与“感受伯尔尼”(Feel The Bern)一样。
邮寄的政治传单通常会指明为文学买单的组织。但“感觉伯尔尼”和其他两个邮递员显然没有提供这些信息。
这些群发邮件都有相同的长滩邮寄地址，即古尔德和奥雷利亚纳有限责任公司的办公室。古尔德和奥雷利亚纳自称是“政治报道专家”。SFGate试图联系该公司，但没有人接电话，语音信箱也满了。
邮递员支持乔·拜登-卡马拉·哈里斯(Joe Biden-Kamala Harris)的选票，法院和大学学区的一些候选人，以及各种提议，包括22项，基于App的司机作为承包商和劳工政策倡议(App-based Drivers as Contractors And Labor Policies Initiative)。
这项措施将允许叫车和送货司机继续被视为独立承包商，尽管有一些新的福利让步。如果失败，这些员工很可能会被认为是有权享受最低工资、加班费、工人补偿、失业保险和带薪病假的工人。
与邮递者有关的洛杉矶顾问蕾妮·纳洪(Renee Nahum)证实，她在忧心忡忡的女性选民指南委员会(Council For Concerned Women Voters Guide)工作的报酬为2万美元。当被要求澄清谁为邮递员买单时，她结束了对话，称她必须接一个重要电话。
天哪，他们自称“感受伯尔尼，进步的选民指南”……优步&莱夫特的政治行动委员会付给他们2万美元，让他们在那里放置22号道具https://t.co/9CzWmaSM0k pic.twitter.com/y7KBrLA4hk。
-克里斯·阿文？‘·🌁(@chrisarvinsf)2020年10月9日。
加州国务卿的Cal-Access数据库显示，Yes on 22 Uber/Lyft PAC为石板邮递员花费了大量资金，其中包括6万美元的“加州选民指南”，48750美元的“加州拉丁裔选民指南”和2万美元的“进步石板”。所有这些都是在同一天-8月28日制作的。
note_en:
Political mailers masquerading as progressive voter guides and endorsing Proposition 22, the initiative backed by Uber and Lyft, are showing up in Southern California voters’ mailboxes.
  The fine print on one mailer says it was prepared by the “Feel the Bern, Progressive Voter Guide,” which is not an actual organization. Neither are the "Council of Concerned Women Voters Guide" nor the "Our Voice, Latino Voter Guide," whose mailers make the same endorsements as Feel the Bern.
  Mailed political fliers typically identify the organization that paid for the literature. But that information was conspicuously absent from Feel the Bern and the other two mailers.
     The mass mailings have the same Long Beach mailing address, which is the office of Gould and Orellana LLC. Gould and Orellana calls itself the “Political Reporting Experts.” SFGATE tried to contact the firm, but no one answered the phone and the voicemail boxes were full.
    The mailers back the Joe Biden-Kamala Harris ticket, a number of candidates for courts and college districts, and various propositions, including 22, the App-Based Drivers as Contractors and Labor Policies Initiative.
   The measure would allow ride-hail and delivery drivers to continue to be treated as independent contractors, although with some new benefit concessions. If it fails, these employees would likely be considered workers entitled to a minimum wage, overtime pay, workers' compensation, unemployment insurance and paid sick leave.
     Renee Nahum, a Los Angeles consultant associated with the mailers, confirmed that she received $20,000 for her work on the Council for Concerned Women Voters Guide. When asked to clarify who paid for the mailer, she ended the conversation, saying she had to take an important call.
   omg they call themselves “feel the bern, progressive voter guide”...and they were paid $20k from Uber & Lyft’s PAC to put prop 22 on there  https://t.co/9CzWmaSM0k  pic.twitter.com/y7KBrLA4hk
— chris arvin ?’• 🌁 (@chrisarvinsf)  October 9, 2020
  The California secretary of state’s Cal-Access database shows numerous  expenditures made by the Yes on 22 Uber/Lyft PAC for slate mailers, including $60,000 for a “California Voter Guide,” $48,750 for a “California Latino Voter Guide” and $20,000 for a “Progressive Slate.” All were made on the same date — Aug. 28.
title: 伪装成进步选民指南并支持由优步(Uber)和Lyft支持的22号提案(Propose22)的邮递员出现在加州的邮箱里
title_en: Mailers masquerading as progressive voter guides and endorsing Proposition 22, the initiative backed by Uber and Lyft, are showing up in California mailboxes

note: 如果你计划在这个节日期间买一部iPhone11，那么你可能会进退两难，不知道该选择哪种优惠。就在亚马逊宣布iPhone11将在10月17日开始的亚马逊大印度节(Amazon Great Indian Festival)促销期间以5万卢比(约合49999卢比)的价格出售几天后，苹果在印度新推出的在线商店也将推出一个有吸引力的报价。苹果将向在印度苹果在线商店购物或直接访问苹果印度网站的iPhone11购买者提供免费的AirPods。排灯节的报价将从10月17日开始。苹果可能还没有披露一些细节，但这一报价将是有限的。买家还将获得苹果公司专门在AirPods、iPad和苹果铅笔上制作的版画。因此，如果你购买iPhone11，你可以免费在AirPods上雕刻。IPhone11的基本机型售价为68300卢比，在苹果在线商店上有64 GB的存储空间。AirPods的售价为14,900卢比。因此，在提供免费AirPods的情况下，iPhone11的有效价格降至53,400卢比。与此同时，亚马逊将对iPhone11提供更多的折扣，在节日销售季节，价格将从68300卢比降至49999卢比。因此，如果你不想要AirPods，从亚马逊购买iPhone11是一笔更好的交易。但是，对于那些以5万卢比从亚马逊购买iPhone11，然后分别从亚马逊或苹果购买AirPods的人来说，iPhone11(64 GB)和AirPods的组合交易价格为6.83万卢比可能并不是最好的。有趣的是，AirPods在亚马逊上的价格已经更低了。对于iPhone11的购买者来说，另一件需要记住的重要事情是，在亚马逊上，iPhone的打折通常不会持续很长时间。因此，你可能不得不抓紧时间，让你的钱赚到最好的钱。
note_en: If you are planning to buy an iPhone 11 during this festive season then you may get into a dilemma regarding which offer to opt for. Just days after Amazon announced that the iPhone 11 will be available under Rs 50,000 (around Rs 49,999) during the Amazon Great Indian Festival sale starting october 17, Apple’s newly launched online store in India is also set to introduce an attractive offer.  Apple will be offering free AirPods to iPhone 11 buyers who shop from the online India Apple store or by simply visiting Apple’s India website. The Diwali offer will start from October 17. There may be some fine print which Apple is yet to disclose but this offer will be for a limited time. What buyers will also get are engravings that Apple does exclusively on the AirPods, iPad and Apple Pencil. So, if you buy the iPhone 11, you can get free engraving on the AirPods.    The iPhone 11 sells at Rs 68,300 for the base variant with 64GB storage on the Apple online store. The AirPods sell at a price of Rs 14,900. So, it brings down the effective price of the iPhone 11 to Rs 53,400 with the free AirPods offer.  Meanwhile, Amazon will be offering a slightly more discount on the iPhone 11 with the price going down from Rs 68,300 to Rs 49,999 during the festive sale season. So, if you do not want AirPods, buying the iPhone 11 from Amazon is a better deal. But the iPhone 11 (64GB) and AirPods combo deal at Rs 68,300 from Apple may not be the best for someone who buys the iPhone 11 from Amazon at Rs 50,000 and buys the AirPods separately from either Amazon or Apple. Interestingly, the price of AirPods is already lower on Amazon.  For iPhone 11 buyers, another important thing to keep in mind is that deals on iPhones usually doesn't last for long on Amazon. So, you may have to hurry to get the best bang for your buck.
title: 作为排灯节促销活动的一部分，苹果将向在印度新开的在线商店购物的iPhone11买家免费提供AirPods
title_en: Apple will offer free AirPods to iPhone 11 buyers who shop from its new online store in India, as part of a promotion for the Diwali festival

note:
Datavant是一家总部位于加利福尼亚州旧金山的解决方案提供商，为生命科学和医疗保健组织提供解决方案，在保护患者隐私的同时连接他们的数据。Datavant在B系列融资中筹集了4000万美元。
这轮融资使Datavant的累计融资达到8300万美元，由Transform Capital牵头，新投资者强生创新-JJDC，Inc.和信诺风险投资公司(Cigna Ventures)参与，罗伊万特科学公司(Roivant Sciences)和Flex Capital的现有股东也参与其中。
该公司打算利用这笔资金继续扩大其开放的医疗数据生态系统。
在创始人兼首席执行官特拉维斯·梅(Travis May)的领导下，Datavant提供技术，帮助数据持有者管理实现安全数据交换所需的隐私、安全、合规和信任。它的技术被350多个机构用来识别他们的数据集，然后这些数据集可以链接到Datavant生态系统内的其他数据集。
宣布与Blue Health Intelligence、Komodo Health、Medable、Parexel、Precision Digital Health、Prognos、Symphony Health、Trialbee和TriNetX建立新的合作伙伴关系，以实现跨分析和制药公司的真实世界数据连接，包括纳入临床试验数据和真实世界数据。
与几个合作伙伴共同推出了中国新冠肺炎研究数据库，这是用于与新冠肺炎相关的学术和医学研究的最大的真实世界数据无偿来源，该数据库拥有1,500多名注册研究人员和100项正在进行的研究。
收购了Highland Math，并推出了Connect Cloud工具套件，专注于消除数据交换中的额外摩擦。
增加了唐·贝里克(CMS前行政长官)、大卫·舒尔金(退伍军人事务部前部长)和安德鲁·冯·埃森巴赫(前FDA专员)为顾问委员会成员。
实施了跨数十个学术医疗中心的链接技术，这些中心构成了以患者为中心的结果研究网络。
该公司的生态系统由350多个机构组成，包括70多个学术医疗中心、制药公司、100多家健康技术和数据公司、健康保险公司、专业药店和患者中心以及实验室。
note_en:
Datavant, a San Francisco, CA-based provider of solutions for life sciences and healthcare organizations to connect their data while protecting patient privacy, raised $40m in Series B funding.
  The round, which brought Datavant’s cumulative fundraising to $83m, was led by Transformation Capital, with participation from new investors Johnson & Johnson Innovation – JJDC, Inc. and Cigna Ventures, as well as participation by existing shareholders Roivant Sciences and Flex Capital.
 The company intends to use the funds to continue to expand its open healthcare data ecosystem.
 Led by Travis May, Founder & CEO, Datavant provides technology to help data holders manage the privacy, security, compliance, and trust required to enable safe data exchange. Its technology is used by more than 350 institutions to de-identify their data sets, which are then linkable to other data sets within the Datavant ecosystem.
  Announced new partnerships with Blue Health Intelligence, Komodo Health, Medable, Parexel, Precision Digital Health, Prognos, Symphony Health, Trialbee, and TriNetX to enable the connection of real-world data across analytics and pharmaceutical companies, including incorporating clinical trial data and real-world data
  Launched the COVID-19 Research Database with several partners, the largest pro bono source of real-world data being used for academic and medical research related to COVID-19, which has more than 1,500 registered researchers and 100 studies in progress
 Acquired Highland Math and launched its Connect Cloud suite of tools focused on removing additional friction from data exchange
 Added Don Berwick (former Administrator of CMS), David Shulkin (former Secretary of the VA), and Andrew von Eschenbach (former FDA Commissioner) to the advisory board
 Implemented linking technology across the many dozens of academic medical centers that comprise the Patient-Centered Outcomes Research Network
 The company’s ecosystem of more than 350 institutions comprises more than 70 academic medical centers, pharmaceutical companies, over 100 health technology and data companies, health insurers, specialty pharmacies and patient hubs, and labs.
title: Datavant允许医疗保健组织在保护患者隐私的同时连接他们的数据，该公司筹集了4000万美元的B系列资金，使其筹资总额达到8300万美元
title_en: Datavant, which lets healthcare organizations connect their data while protecting patient privacy, raises $40M Series B, bringing its total raised to $83M

亚马逊已正式取消其免费多人射击游戏Crucble。这是这款游戏坎坷旅程的结束，这款游戏在经过多年的开发后于5月份首次推出，然后在7月份重新进入封闭测试版。
“我们非常感谢粉丝们团结在我们努力的周围，我们很高兴看到你们对我们在过去几个月里所做的改变的反应，但最终我们并没有看到(坩埚)一个健康、可持续的未来，”坩埚团队在一篇博客文章中说。“我们很高兴看到你们对我们过去几个月所做的改变的反应，但最终我们看不到坩埚有一个健康、可持续的未来。”
如果你处于封闭测试版，你仍然可以在短时间内以某种形式玩这款游戏。根据博客的说法，开发者将在接下来的几周内举办一场“最后的游戏测试和社区庆祝活动”。一旦完成，配对将被禁用，但你将可以玩定制游戏(预计将在未来几天内推出)，直到11月9日美国东部时间下午3点。该公司还表示，对于您可能购买的任何商品，它都将提供全额退款。如果您想要退款，请按照本网站上的说明操作。
Crucble开发者将被转移到其他亚马逊游戏游戏上，包括亚马逊即将推出的MMO“新世界”(New World)。这款游戏目前定于2021年春季推出，它自己也面临着一系列挑战，包括两次推迟。我们还在等待Pac-Man Live Studio的发布，这是一个可以直接在Twitch上播放的Pac-Man版本。亚马逊在5月曾表示，该游戏将在6月推出，但目前仍未推出，游戏官网也只是说“很快就会推出”.。
亚马逊不仅仅是制作游戏-它还推出了云游戏服务，名为露娜，这项服务是在上个月推出的。美国的用户现在就可以申请提前访问它。如果你想了解露娜与其他云游戏产品的对比，我们这里有一个指南。
note_en:
Amazon has officially canceled  Crucible, its free-to-play multiplayer shooter. It’s the end of a rocky journey for the game, which was first launched  in May after years in development before being put back into closed beta  in July.
 “We very much appreciate the way that our fans have rallied around our efforts, and we’ve loved seeing your responses to the changes we’ve made over the last few months, but ultimately we didn’t see a healthy, sustainable future ahead [for]  Crucible,” the  Crucible team said  in a blog post.
 If you’re in the closed beta, you’ll still be able to play the game in some form for a short while. The developers will be hosting a “a final playtest and community celebration” in the next few weeks, according to the blog. Once that’s done, matchmaking will be disabled, but you’ll be able to play custom games (which are expected to be available in the coming days) until 3PM ET on November 9th. The company also says that it will be offering a full refund for any purchases you might have made. If you want refunds, follow the instructions on  this site.
  Crucible developers will be moved to other Amazon Games titles, including  New World, Amazon’s upcoming MMO. That game, which is currently set to launch in spring 2021, has had its own set of challenges, including  two delays. And we’re also still waiting on the release of  Pac-Man Live Studio, a version of Pac-Man that you can play directly on Twitch. Amazon said  in May that the game would launch in June, but it’s still not out, and the game’s website only says that it’s “ coming soon.”
 Amazon isn’t just making games — it also has cloud gaming service  called Luna that it launched last month. Users in the US can request early access to it right now. If you want to understand how Luna stacks up against other cloud gaming offerings, we’ve got a guide for that  right here.
title: 亚马逊取消了其免费多人射击游戏Crucble，此前该游戏在7月份因负面评论而重新进入封闭测试版
title_en: Amazon has canceled Crucible, its free-to-play multiplayer shooter game, after putting it back in closed beta in July amid negative reviews

数字精神健康公司大脑宣布，它完成了3500万美元的首轮融资。这笔投资由Oak HC/FT牵头，Westcap、Liquid 2 Ventures、Gaingels、Air Angels和其他未具名的投资者参与其中。
Brain是一项会员服务，为客户提供与提供者的远程医疗访问，并每月与护理顾问聊天。在客户签约使用这项服务后，他们会与提供者和护理顾问配对。提供者能够提出治疗计划，其中可以包括药物治疗。
作为这项服务的一部分，该公司可以将客户的药物邮寄给客户。在最初的咨询之后，这项服务为客户提供了随着时间的推移与提供商聊天和签到的能力。该公司还提供了一款应用程序，帮助患者每月跟踪和报告症状。
该计划的药物和基本护理套餐每月99美元，如果患者选择每周去看治疗师，而不是每月去看他们的护理顾问，每月399美元，每周与有执照的治疗师进行会谈，每月259美元。它符合FSA/HAS资格。据该公司称，其服务面向患有焦虑、抑郁和失眠的个人。
该公司计划利用这笔新资金将业务扩展到所有50个州。这3500万美元还将用于与保险公司建立合作伙伴关系，并开发一款能够支持客户护理需求的应用程序。
在数字健康中，心理健康空间炙手可热。2020年上半年，The Rock Health报告称，数字行为健康公司获得了高达5.88亿美元的融资。其中几家公司获得了超过1亿美元的资金。医疗应用程序Headspace以1.007亿美元的股权完成了C系列融资。
今年5月，Mindstrong获得了1亿美元的融资。Mindstrong是一家为支付者组织提供服务的公司，为其成员提供服务。这项服务允许患者访问与提供商的视频和电话交谈，并可以帮助跟踪数字生物标记物。
行为健康领域的其他主要参与者包括2019年获得5000万美元收入的Talkspace；今年早些时候锁定了3900万美元B系列融资的Big Health；以及去年与沃尔玛达成交易的SilverCloud。
Oak HC/FT负责人比利·戴奇(Billy Deitch)在一份声明中表示：“通过积极主动的管理和跟踪，CBrain已经弥合了临床护理方面的差距，并减少了将患者连接到他们所需的专业精神医疗服务的障碍。”CBrain正在应对市场上快速增长的需求，消费者渴望使用这些服务。我们很高兴看到CBrain公司如何继续为医疗保健行业带来革命性的变化，并影响远程医疗的采用。“
note_en:
Digital mental health company Cerebral announced that it closed its Series A funding round with $35 million. The investment was led by Oak HC/FT with participation from Westcap, Liquid 2 Ventures, Gaingels, Air Angels and other unnamed investors.
  Cerebral is a membership service that offers clients telehealth visits with a provider and monthly chats with a care counselor. After a client signs up for the service they are matched up with a provider and a care counselor. The provider is able to come up with a treatment plan, which can include medication.
 As part of the service, the company can ship clients their medications in the mail. After the initial consultation the service offers clients the ability to chat and check in with the provider over time. The company also offers an app that helps patient track and report symptoms on a monthly basis.
 The plan is $99 a month for the medication and basic care package, $399 a month if a patient chooses to see a therapist weekly instead of having monthly visits with their care counselor, and $259 a month for weekly sessions with a licensed therapist. It is FSA/HAS eligible. According to the company its services are geared towards individuals living with anxiety, depression and insomnia.
  The company plans to use the new money to expand into all 50 states. The $35 million will also go towards making partnerships with insurers and building out an app that can support the clients care needs.
  The mental health space is hot in digital health. In the first half of 2020,  Rock Health reported that digital behavioral health companies scored a whopping $588 million in funding. Several of these companies received over $100 million. Medication app  Headspace finished its Series C raise with $100.7 million in equity.
 In May  Mindstrong, a company that caters to payer organizations to offer services to its members, scored $100 million in funding. The service lets patients access video and phone conversations with providers and can help track digital biomarkers.
 Other major player in the behavior health space include  Talkspace, which scored $50 million in 2019;  Big Health, which locked in $39 million in Series B funding earlier this year; and  SilverCloud, which made a deal with Walmart last year.
  “Through proactive management and tracking, Cerebral has bridged gaps in clinical care and reduced barriers to connecting patients to the specialized mental healthcare they need,” Billy Deitch, principal at Oak HC/FT, said in a statement. “Cerebral is tackling a fast-growing need in the market, and consumers are eager to use these services. We’re thrilled to see how Cerebral continues to revolutionize the healthcare industry and shape telehealth adoption.”
title: 数字心理健康订阅服务CBrain筹集3500万美元A系列资金，由Oak HC/FT牵头
title_en: Digital mental health subscription service Cerebral raises $35M Series A led by Oak HC/FT

Onapsis是一家总部位于波士顿的公司，为任务关键型应用程序提供网络安全和合规解决方案。该公司周二宣布，已在D系列融资中筹集了5500万美元。
最新一笔投资使该公司筹集的资金总额达到1.17亿美元，由魁北克Caisse de dépôt et Placement du Québec(CDPQ)和NightDragon牵头，现有投资者参与其中。406风险投资公司、LLR Partners和阿森纳风险投资伙伴公司。
该公司周二还宣布支持Salesforce和SuccessFtors，并已启动了Salesforce的Onapsis平台和SuccessFators的Onapsis平台的早期访问计划。
Onapsis表示，它将利用新筹集的资金，通过快速扩张到任务关键型SaaS应用程序市场，从保护Salesforce和SuccessFtors应用程序开始，显著扩大公司的规模。
该公司提供了一个平台，使组织能够识别、区分优先级并解决可能对敏感系统和数据构成威胁的漏洞、错误配置和恶意活动。
在接下来的几个月里，它计划推出一项针对Oracle HCM云、Oracle ERP云、Workday和其他SaaS应用程序的早期访问计划支持。
Onapsis声称其年度经常性收入增长了145%以上，并表示其客户保留率为98%。该公司表示，300个主要品牌正在使用其解决方案，其中包括20%的财富100强公司。
Onapsis还以其研究团队而闻名，他们发现了800多个漏洞，主要存在于SAP和甲骨文的产品中。
note_en:
Onapsis, a Boston-based company that provides cybersecurity and compliance solutions for mission-critical applications, announced on Tuesday that it has raised $55 million in a Series D funding round.
 The latest investment, which brings the total  raised by the company to $117 million, was led by Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec (CDPQ) and NightDragon, with participation from existing investors .406 Ventures, LLR Partners and Arsenal Venture Partners.
 The company on Tuesday also announced support for Salesforce and SuccessFactors, and that it has launched an early access program for the Onapsis Platform for Salesforce and the Onapsis Platform for SuccessFactors.
 Onapsis says it will use the newly raised money to “significantly scale the company through rapid expansion into the mission-critical SaaS applications market, starting with protection and compliance for Salesforce and SuccessFactors applications.”
 The company provides a platform that enables organizations to identify, prioritize and address vulnerabilities, misconfigurations and malicious activity that can pose a threat to sensitive systems and data.
 In the coming months, it plans on launching an early access program support for Oracle HCM Cloud, Oracle ERP Cloud, Workday and other SaaS applications.
 Onapsis claims its annual recurring revenue has increased by over 145%, and says it has a 98% customer retention rate. The company says 300 major brands are using its solutions, including 20% of the Fortune 100 companies.
 Onapsis is also known for its research team, which has discovered more than 800 vulnerabilities, mainly in products from  SAP and  Oracle.
title: 为SAP、甲骨文和Salesforce提供网络安全和合规服务的Onapsis筹集了5500万美元的D系列资金，使其筹资总额达到1.17亿美元
title_en: Onapsis, which provides cybersecurity and compliance services for SAP, Oracle, and Salesforce, raises $55M Series D, bringing its total raised to $117M
